causeBreakdown.source: WTSC 2023 Annual Traffic Fatalities Report (WA state-level)
causeBreakdown.note: Washington state-level percentages; categories overlap (a crash can involve multiple factors). Seattle-specific breakdowns may differ.

rideHailVMTShare.note: Seattle metro ride-hail ~1.9% of VMT (lowest of 6 cities studied). The 5% figure assumes AV ride-hail captures the existing market in the urban core and grows significantly, as it has in other cities.

waymo.source: Kusano et al., Traffic Injury Prevention (2025), 56.7M miles

rideHailSafetyComparison.caveat: Methodologies differ across these sources. Waymo data uses police-reported incidents matched to location baselines; Uber/Lyft figures are self-reported. Direct comparison should be interpreted with caution.

studies.name: Kusano et al. (2024)
studies.finding: 85% fewer any-injury-reported crashes over 7.1M autonomous miles

studies.name: Kusano et al. (2025)
studies.finding: 85% fewer suspected serious injury+ crashes, 79% fewer any-injury-reported crashes over 56.7M miles

studies.name: Swiss Re / Waymo (2024)
studies.finding: Waymo vehicles had zero bodily injury claims vs. human baseline

permitTimeline.note: Testing since Sep 2025 — no legal pathway to commercial service

timeline.detail: Promotes autonomous vehicle testing and technology development in Washington
Establishes AV testing framework — but no pathway to commercial driverless service
First legislative session after RCW 46.92 — opportunity to enable commercial AV deployment
Bill to allow fully driverless AV operation fails to pass
Bill would require a licensed human driver in all AVs — moving backward
Safety drivers required — data collection only, no public rides
Restrictive bill retained — legislature still moving away from AV deployment
No commercial driverless AV service possible under Washington law

sources.name: NHTSA FARS
sources.description: Fatality Analysis Reporting System — national traffic fatality data

sources.name: SDOT Vision Zero
sources.description: Seattle Department of Transportation traffic safety initiative and crash data

sources.name: WTSC Traffic Fatalities Report
sources.description: Washington Traffic Safety Commission — statewide fatality data and cause breakdowns

sources.name: RCW 46.92 — Autonomous Motor Vehicles
sources.description: Washington state law establishing AV testing framework (no commercial pathway)

sources.name: HB 1984 — Require Human Driver in AVs
sources.description: Proposed Washington legislation that would mandate a licensed human driver in all autonomous vehicles

sources.name: Executive Order 17-02
sources.description: Gov. Inslee's 2017 executive order promoting autonomous vehicle testing in Washington

sources.name: Kusano et al. (2024)
sources.description: Peer-reviewed study: 85% fewer any-injury-reported crashes in 7.1M Waymo miles

sources.name: Kusano et al. (2025) — 56.7M Miles
sources.description: Updated peer-reviewed study: 85% fewer suspected serious injury+ crashes over 56.7M Waymo miles

sources.name: Swiss Re / Waymo Insurance Study (2024)
sources.description: Zero bodily injury claims for Waymo fleet vs. human baseline

sources.name: Fehr & Peers Ride-Hail VMT Study (2019)
sources.description: Ride-hail as % of VMT across major US cities (Seattle metro: 1.9%, SF: 12.8%)

sources.name: Waymo Seattle Testing Announcement
sources.description: Waymo begins deploying test vehicles in Seattle (Sep 2025)
